text.title: Runbook as Code Pattern Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every runbook-as-code adoption must enforce so on-call always lands on the right step and rotted runbooks fail CI, not pagers.

rule.statement: Each service's runbook lives in that service's repo at a stable path (default: `docs/runbook.md` or `runbooks/<alert>.md`). Runbooks stored in a separate "ops wiki" without a copy in the repo are rejected as not-runbook-as-code regardless of how good the content is.
rule.rationale: Wiki runbooks fall out of date because they are never reviewed when the code changes. Co-location means the service PR that breaks the runbook is the same PR that must update it, and the on-call only ever needs one URL to find current content.

rule.statement: Every alert rule has a dedicated anchor in the runbook (`#alert-rule-slug`). The runbook frontmatter must list `alert_anchors:` as a map of alert-rule-slug → anchor. An alert without a corresponding anchor is rejected at CI; an anchor without a corresponding alert is allowed but flagged for cleanup.
rule.rationale: On-call woken at 03:00 should not skim a 40-section runbook. Anchor-per-alert means the pager link lands on the exact step matching the rule that fired. The frontmatter map makes the mapping checkable, not implicit.

rule.statement: CI must run a fast check (target <60s) that: (a) every alert_anchors entry resolves to an existing anchor in the runbook body, (b) every external URL in the runbook returns 200/301/302, (c) every shell snippet's binary exists in the repo's documented prereq list. A failing check blocks merge of the service repo.
rule.rationale: Link rot, missing anchors, and references to retired CLIs are the three most common ways a "real" runbook becomes a 03:00 dead-end. Catching them in CI converts runbook quality from a periodic audit into a per-commit gate.

rule.statement: Every alert rule emitted by the service must carry a `runbook_url` field pointing at the anchor-suffixed URL (e.g. `https://github.com/org/svc/blob/main/docs/runbook.md#alert-payment-error-rate-high`). Alerts without runbook_url, or with a URL whose anchor does not exist (per r3), are rejected at the alert-rule PR stage.
rule.rationale: An alert without a runbook link is hostile to the responder. The "URL must include a valid anchor" requirement closes the gap where a generic /runbook URL is technically present but useless because the responder still has to hunt.

rule.statement: Pull requests that change service behaviour related to a documented alert (new failure mode, retry-policy change, dependency swap) must also update the corresponding runbook section, or carry an explicit `runbook-unchanged: <reason>` label. CODEOWNERS for runbook files must overlap with service CODEOWNERS — a separate "docs team" reviewer is forbidden for runbook changes.
rule.rationale: Splitting code review and runbook review across teams creates a permanent backlog where the runbook trails the code by months. Forcing the same reviewers to see both keeps the two in lockstep, and the explicit `runbook-unchanged` label preserves the audit trail for genuinely no-op changes.

example.description: Service runbook frontmatter wired to alerts and CI.
example.code: --- service: checkout-api alert_anchors: payment-error-rate-high: "#alert-payment-error-rate-high" upstream-timeout-spike: "#alert-upstream-timeout-spike" queue-depth-saturation: "#alert-queue-depth-saturation" ci_link_check: required last_reviewed_with_release: v2.14.0 --- # checkout-api runbook ## alert-payment-error-rate-high **Symptom:** error rate > 2% on /charge for 5 minutes. **First check:** `kubectl logs -l app=checkout-api --since=10m | rg ERR` ...
